bullets: AI Inventory and System Registry Checklist
sections.heading: AI Governance Checklist Templates:
paragraphs: Purpose: Use this checklist to create and maintain a complete, current registry of all AI systems, use cases, tools, models, and AI-enabled vendor features in the organization.
Registry Scope: ☐ Enterprise-wide ☐ Business Unit ☐ Pilot Program ☐ Other: ___________

sections.heading: 1) Registry Governance Setup
paragraphs:
☐ Registry owner assigned
☐ Registry backup owner assigned
☐ Standard registry fields defined and approved
☐ Update cadence defined (monthly/quarterly)
☐ Submission process defined for new AI use cases
☐ Change/update process defined for existing entries
☐ Decommissioning/removal process defined
☐ Source systems identified for reconciliation (procurement, app catalog, SSO, cloud, vendor list)

sections.heading: 2) Required Record Identity Fields
paragraphs: For each AI entry, confirm the record includes:

☐ Unique AI Use Case ID / Registry ID
☐ Use Case Name
☐ Business Unit / Department
☐ Business Owner
☐ Technical Owner / Platform Owner
☐ Vendor / Tool Name (if third-party)
☐ Model Name / Version (if known)
☐ Environment (Pilot / Test / Production / Retired)
☐ Current Status (Proposed / In Review / Approved / Active / Suspended / Retired)

sections.heading: 3) Business Context Fields
paragraphs:
☐ Business purpose documented
☐ Problem statement documented
☐ Intended users identified (employees/customers/vendors)
☐ Internal-only vs customer-facing identified
☐ Decision impact level identified (informational / advisory / decision support / automated)
☐ Criticality to operations identified (low/med/high)

sections.heading: 4) Technical and Architecture Fields
paragraphs:
☐ AI type identified (GenAI / Predictive / Classification / Agentic / Other)
☐ Deployment model identified (SaaS / API / On-prem / Cloud-hosted)
☐ Hosting region / data residency captured (if applicable)
☐ Integrations listed (apps, APIs, databases, connectors)
☐ Read/write capability identified
☐ Authentication method captured (SSO/MFA/local accounts)
☐ Logging capability identified
☐ Known dependencies documented

sections.heading: 5) Data and Privacy Fields
paragraphs:
☐ Data types processed documented (public/internal/confidential)
☐ Personal data involvement identified (PII)
☐ Sensitive/regulated data involvement identified (PHI/PCI/financial/minors)
☐ Data source(s) identified
☐ Cross-border data transfer flag captured
☐ Data retention/storage behavior captured
☐ Vendor training/retention terms captured (if third-party AI)
☐ Privacy review required flag captured
☐ DPIA/PIA required flag captured

sections.heading: 6) Risk and Compliance Fields
paragraphs:
☐ Risk tier assigned (Low / Moderate / High / Critical)
☐ Risk assessment reference ID linked
☐ Compliance risk register reference linked (if applicable)
☐ Applicable regulations/policies listed
☐ Security review status captured
☐ Legal review status captured
☐ Vendor risk review status captured
☐ Exceptions logged (if any) with expiry date
☐ Approval status and approval date captured

sections.heading: 7) Evidence and Traceability Fields
paragraphs:
☐ Intake form link stored
☐ Risk assessment link stored
☐ Approval record link stored
☐ Evidence pack link stored (if required)
☐ Monitoring plan link stored
☐ Owner contact details current
☐ Source of truth location identified (SharePoint/GRC/Jira/etc.)

sections.heading: 8) Lifecycle and Monitoring Fields
paragraphs:
☐ Go-live date recorded
☐ Next review / revalidation date recorded
☐ Monitoring owner assigned
☐ Revalidation frequency recorded
☐ Last monitoring review date recorded
☐ Last material change date recorded
☐ Retirement/decommission date recorded (if retired)
☐ Reason for retirement documented (if applicable)

sections.heading: 9) Registry Reconciliation and Completeness Checks
paragraphs:
☐ Registry reconciled against procurement/vendor list
☐ Registry reconciled against SSO/IdP app catalog
☐ Registry reconciled against cloud/API inventory (if applicable)
☐ Registry reconciled against business-submitted use cases
☐ Unapproved AI tools discovered and flagged
☐ Duplicate entries removed/merged
☐ Retired tools archived from active list
☐ Missing owners remediated
☐ Missing risk tiers remediated

sections.heading: 10) Registry Quality Checks
paragraphs:
☐ No blank critical fields (owner, purpose, status, risk tier)
☐ Dates are current and valid
☐ Entries have clear lifecycle status
☐ High-risk entries include linked assessments and approvals
☐ Exceptions have active expiry dates and owners
☐ Evidence links are accessible and not broken
Registry Quality Status: ☐ Complete ☐ Partial ☐ Needs Cleanup

sections.heading: 11) Governance Sign-Off
paragraphs:
Registry Reviewed By (AI Governance): __________________ Date: ________
Registry Reviewed By (Security/GRC): __________________ Date: ________
Registry Approved for Reporting: ______________________ Date: ________

bullets: AI Third-Party and Vendor Due Diligence Checklist
sections.heading: Quick Tips
paragraphs: Treat the registry like a system of record, not a one-time spreadsheet.
If a tool has AI features enabled, it belongs in the registry—even if “we’re just testing.”
Reconcile against SSO and procurement regularly; shadow AI will not volunteer itself.
Purpose: Use this checklist to assess AI vendors, AI-enabled SaaS features, model/API providers, and third-party AI services before approval and during renewals.

Vendor Name: ____________________________________
Product / Service: ________________________________
Business Sponsor: _________________________________
Vendor Owner (Internal): __________________________
Assessment Date: __________________________________
Assessment Type: ☐ New Vendor ☐ New AI Feature ☐ Renewal ☐ Material Change Review

sections.heading: 1) Vendor and Service Overview
paragraphs:
☐ Vendor legal entity name confirmed
☐ Product/service name and scope documented
☐ AI capability described clearly (what it does)
☐ Intended internal use documented
☐ Customer-facing use documented (if applicable)
☐ Business criticality assigned (low/med/high)
☐ Deployment model identified (SaaS / API / hosted / embedded feature)

sections.heading: 2) AI-Specific Product and Model Details
paragraphs:
☐ AI type identified (GenAI / predictive / classification / agentic)
☐ Model source identified (vendor-built / third-party model / OSS / multiple)
☐ Model versioning approach documented
☐ Vendor discloses material model changes/updates process
☐ Human oversight features available (review/approval controls)
☐ Output controls available (filters, moderation, guardrails)
☐ Logging and audit features available
☐ Explainability/transparency information available (as applicable)

sections.heading: 3) Data Handling and Privacy Review
paragraphs:
☐ Data types to be processed documented
☐ Personal data involved identified
☐ Sensitive/regulated data use identified
☐ Data flow documented (input, processing, storage, output)
☐ Data residency / hosting region confirmed
☐ Cross-border transfer implications reviewed
☐ Data retention periods documented
☐ Vendor confirms whether customer data is used for model training
☐ Opt-out / no-training options available and configured (if needed)
☐ Telemetry/logging data usage documented
☐ Data deletion process and timelines documented
☐ DPA/privacy addendum reviewed

sections.heading: 4) Security and Technical Controls
paragraphs:
☐ SSO supported
☐ MFA supported/enforced for admin access
☐ Role-based access control available
☐ Least-privilege roles configurable
☐ Encryption in transit and at rest documented
☐ API authentication and key management controls documented
☐ Audit logging available and exportable
☐ Security incident notification commitments documented
☐ Vulnerability management process described
☐ Penetration testing / independent assessments available
☐ Secure SDLC practices described
☐ Secrets handling and tenant isolation documented (if relevant)
Evidence Collected: ☐ SOC 2 ☐ ISO 27001 ☐ Pen Test Summary ☐ Security Whitepaper ☐ Other: _______

sections.heading: 5) Compliance and Regulatory Alignment
paragraphs:
☐ Applicable regulations identified (privacy, sector, AI-related, cyber)
☐ Vendor supports customer compliance obligations (logging, export, deletion, access control)
☐ Contract includes required security/privacy terms
☐ Audit/assessment rights addressed
☐ Subprocessor list provided and reviewed
☐ Change notification obligations included
☐ Record retention / eDiscovery support assessed (if applicable)
☐ Contractual limitations/risk accepted by Legal/Compliance (if any)

sections.heading: 6) AI Safety and Misuse Controls
paragraphs:
☐ Prompt injection / malicious input protections documented (if applicable)
☐ Output filtering / content safety controls documented
☐ Abuse monitoring and rate-limiting controls documented
☐ High-risk use restrictions or prohibited uses documented
☐ User/admin controls to disable risky features available
☐ Fallback behavior documented for system failure / degraded output

sections.heading: 7) Operational Resilience and Support
paragraphs:
☐ SLA / uptime commitments reviewed
☐ Support model and escalation contacts documented
☐ Business continuity / disaster recovery posture reviewed
☐ Incident response integration points identified
☐ Change management / release cadence documented
☐ Vendor roadmap dependencies identified (if business-critical)

sections.heading: 8) Commercial and Contractual Review
paragraphs:
☐ Pricing model understood (users, tokens, calls, tiers)
☐ Renewal and termination terms reviewed
☐ Data export and exit support documented
☐ Liability / indemnity terms reviewed (including IP issues, if applicable)
☐ Usage restrictions and acceptable use terms reviewed
☐ Procurement approval complete

sections.heading: 9) Internal Risk Decision and Conditions
paragraphs:
☐ Vendor risk rating assigned (Low / Moderate / High / Critical)
☐ AI-specific risk concerns documented
☐ Required compensating controls documented
☐ Approval conditions documented (e.g., no sensitive data, pilot only, logging required)
☐ Monitoring cadence assigned
☐ Reassessment trigger criteria documented (new features, model changes, incidents, contract renewal)
Decision: ☐ Approve ☐ Approve with Conditions ☐ Defer ☐ Reject

sections.heading: 10) Evidence Pack for Vendor Review
paragraphs:
☐ Security documentation collected
☐ Privacy/DPA documentation collected
☐ Subprocessor documentation collected
☐ Contract redlines/approvals stored
☐ Internal review notes stored
☐ Final decision record stored
☐ Owner and next review date recorded

sections.heading: Quick Tips
paragraphs: The most important AI vendor question is often simple: “Will you use our data to train your models?”
Treat “embedded AI features” in existing tools as new risk, not “already approved because we bought the platform.”
Approval conditions are powerful—use them (pilot-only, no PII, restricted users, logging mandatory).

sections.heading: 3. AI Monitoring and Revalidation Checklist
paragraphs: Purpose: Use this checklist to monitor AI use cases after launch, detect control drift or risk changes, and perform periodic revalidation for continued approval.

Use Case Name: ____________________________________
Registry ID: _______________________________________
Business Owner: ____________________________________
Monitoring Owner: __________________________________
Risk Tier: ☐ Low ☐ Moderate ☐ High ☐ Critical
Environment: ☐ Pilot ☐ Production
Review Type: ☐ Scheduled Review ☐ Triggered Revalidation ☐ Post-Incident Review
Review Date: _______________________________________

sections.heading: 1) Review Scope and Trigger
paragraphs:
☐ Review period defined
☐ Review type identified (scheduled vs triggered)
☐ Trigger reason documented (if triggered)
☐ Current risk tier confirmed
☐ Current version/model/vendor version confirmed
☐ Monitoring and revalidation owners confirmed

Triggered Revalidation Reasons (check if applicable):
☐ Material model/version change
☐ Vendor feature change
☐ New data source added
☐ New integration / write access added
☐ Change in business purpose
☐ Incident / near miss / harmful output
☐ Regulatory change
☐ Audit finding / exception expiry
☐ Performance degradation / drift signal

sections.heading: 2) Business and Use Case Continuity Review
paragraphs:
☐ Use case purpose still valid and approved
☐ Actual use remains within approved scope
☐ User population unchanged (or changes documented)
☐ Customer-facing status unchanged (or changes reviewed)
☐ Business owner confirms continued need
☐ Any unauthorized use expansion identified and documented

sections.heading: 3) Performance and Quality Monitoring
paragraphs:
☐ Monitoring metrics defined and available
☐ Performance against thresholds reviewed
☐ Accuracy/relevance/quality metrics reviewed (as applicable)
☐ Error rates reviewed
☐ Hallucination or invalid output trends reviewed (GenAI)
☐ Failure modes and edge-case incidents reviewed
☐ User complaints/feedback trends reviewed
☐ Performance degradation/drift assessed (if applicable)

sections.heading: 4) Security Monitoring Review
paragraphs:
☐ Access rights reviewed (least privilege maintained)
☐ Admin accounts reviewed
☐ SSO/MFA controls still enforced
☐ Audit logs available and retained
☐ Suspicious activity/events reviewed
☐ API key/secret rotation status reviewed
☐ DLP alerts or sensitive prompt/output events reviewed
☐ Prompt injection / abuse attempts reviewed (if applicable)
☐ Security incidents or near misses reviewed

sections.heading: 5) Privacy and Data Protection Monitoring
paragraphs:
☐ Data types processed remain within approved scope
☐ New personal/sensitive data usage identified (if any)
☐ Data retention settings reviewed
☐ Data deletion / purge obligations functioning
☐ Cross-border processing remains approved
☐ Vendor training/retention settings unchanged and verified
☐ Privacy incidents/concerns reviewed
☐ DPIA/PIA assumptions still valid (if applicable)

sections.heading: 6) Compliance and Control Monitoring
paragraphs:
☐ Control requirements for current risk tier still implemented
☐ Approval conditions still being followed
☐ Exceptions reviewed (active/expired/overdue)
☐ Evidence links current and accessible
☐ Policy/standard changes assessed for impact
☐ Compliance risk register entry updated (if needed)
☐ Audit findings/remediation actions reviewed
☐ Regulatory change impact assessed (if applicable)

sections.heading: 7) Vendor and Third-Party Change Monitoring
paragraphs:
☐ Vendor release notes reviewed (material changes identified)
☐ Contract/SLA changes reviewed (if any)
☐ Subprocessor changes reviewed
☐ Vendor incidents/outages reviewed
☐ Security/compliance attestations refreshed (if due)
☐ Vendor risk rating remains valid
☐ New vendor restrictions or terms changes assessed

sections.heading: 8) Human Oversight and Operations Review
paragraphs:
☐ Human reviewer roles still assigned and active
☐ Escalation path remains functional
☐ Override/fallback process tested or validated
☐ User training refresh completed (if due)
☐ Operational runbook/contact list current
☐ Staffing or ownership gaps identified and addressed

sections.heading: 9) Revalidation Decision Checklist
paragraphs:
☐ Risk tier remains appropriate
☐ Existing approvals remain valid
☐ Additional review required (Security / Privacy / Legal / Vendor Risk)
☐ New/updated artifacts required (threat model, DPIA, evaluation plan, etc.)
☐ Conditions need to be added/updated
☐ Use case should be restricted, paused, or retired

Revalidation Outcome:
☐ Continue as Approved
☐ Continue with Conditions
☐ Reassess Before Continued Use
☐ Suspend / Pause
☐ Retire Use Case

sections.heading: 11) Evidence and Audit Trail
paragraphs:
☐ Monitoring report attached or linked
☐ KPI/KRI snapshots linked
☐ Incident summaries linked (if any)
☐ Updated risk assessment/risk register link provided (if changed)
☐ Revalidation decision record stored
☐ Next review date set in registry and tracking system

sections.heading: Recommended Review Frequency by Tier
paragraphs: Low: Annual
Moderate: Semi-Annual
High: Quarterly
Critical: Continuous monitoring + Quarterly formal revalidation

sections.heading: Quick Tips
paragraphs: Monitoring is where governance becomes real. Intake gets applause; monitoring prevents headlines.
Trigger-based revalidation is just as important as calendar-based reviews.
If the vendor changed the model and nobody reviewed the impact, your “approval” is stale.
